message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Introduction</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The novel coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) has rapidly spread across the globe, overwhelming healthcare systems and depleting resources. The infection has a wide spectrum of presentations, and pre-existing comorbidities have been found to have a dramatic effect on the disease course and prognosis. We sought to analyze the effect of asthma on the disease progression and outcomes of COVID-19 patients.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We conducted a multi-center retrospective study of positively confirmed COVID-19 patients from multiple hospitals in Louisiana. Demographics, medical history, comorbidities, clinical presentation, daily laboratory values, complications, and outcomes data were collected and analyzed. The primary outcome of interest was in-hospital mortality. Secondary outcomes were Intensive Care Unit (ICU) admission, risk of intubation, duration of mechanical ventilation, and length of hospital stay.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A total of 502 COVID-19 patients (72 asthma and 430 non-asthma cohorts) were included in the study. The frequency of asthma in hospitalized cohorts was 14.3%, higher than the national prevalence of asthma (7.7%). Univariate analysis revealed that asthma patients were more likely to be obese (75% vs 54.2%,
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.001), with higher frequency of intubation (40.3% vs 27.8%,
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    = 0.036), and required longer duration of hospitalization (15.1±12.5 vs 11.5±10.6,
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.015). After adjustment, multivariable analysis showed that asthmatic patients were not associated with higher risk of ICU admission (OR=1.81, 95%CI=0.98-3.09,
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.06), endotracheal intubation (OR=1.77, 95%CI=0.99-3.04,
                    <jats:italic>p=</jats:italic>
                    0.06) or complications (OR=1.37, 95%CI=0.82-2.31,
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.23). Asthmatic patients were not associated with higher odds of prolonged hospital length of stay (OR=1.48, 95%CI=0.82-2.66, p=0.20) or with the duration of ICU stay (OR=0.76, 95%CI=0.28-2.02,
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.58). Kaplan-Meier curve showed no significant difference in overall survival of the two groups (
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.65).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Despite the increased prevalence of hospitalization in asthmatic COVID-19 patients compared to the general population, after adjustment for other variables, it was neither associated with increased severity nor worse outcomes.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Asthma in COVID-19: An extra chain fitting around the neck?

reference.unstructured: Hick, J. L. , D. Hanfling , M. K. Wynia , and A. T. Pavia . 2020. Duty to Plan: Health Care, Crisis Standards of Care, and Novel Coronavirus SARS-CoV-2. NAM Perspectives. Discussion paper. National Academy of Medicine. Washington, DC. https://doi.org/10.31478/202003b

Centers for Disease Control and prevention. Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19): are you at higher risk for severe illness? Atlanta, GA: US Department of Health and Human Services, CDC; 2020. https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/specific-groups/high-risk-complications.html
Summary Health Statistics Tables for U.S. Adults: National Health Interview Survey, 2018, tables A-2b, A-2c. https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/fastats/asthma.htm (Last accessed July first)
Weir CB , Jan A. BMI classification percentile and cut off points. StatPearls. 2019.
